1kW15d8AAAAJ.keywords: software engineering, science policy, articificial intelligence, software reuse, requirements engineering, software design, software maintenance, software engineering education, systems engineering, bioinformatics
1kW15d8AAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Peter A. Freeman is a senior computer scientist affiliated with the **College of Computing, Georgia Tech** and with past appointments at **UC Irvine**. He is associated with research groups that span *software engineering research*, *design science*, and *computing policy* (see his homepage: http://www.cc.gatech.edu/people/peter-freeman). Freeman’s primary research focus is on the theory and practice of <u>software design</u> and <u>software reuse</u>, the intersection of <u>requirements engineering</u> and stakeholder viewpoints, and the application of **artificial intelligence** techniques to software engineering problems. His work bridges rigorous conceptual models of design with pragmatic concerns about workforce supply and science policy, emphasizing both *technical foundations* and *societal impact*.

---

## Research Areas
Freeman’s scholarship centers on mature, foundational topics in **software engineering** and design. A persistent theme is <u>reusability</u>: he developed faceted classification schemes and reusable-information taxonomies in papers such as “Classifying software for reusability” (1987) and “Reusable software engineering: Concepts and research directions” (1983). He has advanced methods for early validation of requirements by explicitly modeling and reconciling multiple stakeholder perspectives, exemplified by “Requirements validation through viewpoint resolution” (1991). Freeman has also contributed to formal and conceptual treatments of design processes — for example, “A model for functional reasoning in design” (1971) and “The nature of design” (1980) — arguing for analytic, teachable models of design for software‑intensive systems. Complementing these technical threads are contributions to *software engineering education* and workforce/policy studies (e.g., “A proposed curriculum for software engineering education” (1978) and “The Supply of Information Technology Workers in the United States” (1999)), and explorations of how **AI** can incrementally assist software engineering tasks (“A framework for incremental progress in the application of AI to software engineering” (1988)). Across these areas Freeman combines empirical, theoretical, and policy-oriented perspectives to address engineering, educational, and societal dimensions of computing.

---

## Notable Works
- “Classifying software for reusability” (1987) — introduced a practical faceted classification scheme that substantially influenced how software components are cataloged and retrieved for reuse (1171 citations).
- “Requirements validation through viewpoint resolution” (1991) — proposed Viewpoint Resolution (VPR) as a structured approach to detect and reconcile divergent stakeholder requirements early in the lifecycle (367 citations).
- “The Supply of Information Technology Workers in the United States” (1999) — a policy‑oriented study linking workforce supply/demand analysis with political and educational contexts, informing debates on IT workforce planning and science policy (355 citations).

---

## Academic Background
Over several decades Freeman has built a prolific and widely cited research portfolio (total citations ≈ **4,892**) spanning software design theory, reuse, requirements engineering, education, and computing policy. He has held academic appointments at **Georgia Tech** and **UC Irvine**, contributed to foundational curriculum proposals and educational workshops, and participated in scholarship that intersects with national research policy (see his organizational history work on the National Science Foundation). His publication record includes influential tutorials, conceptual analyses, and interdisciplinary collaborations that have informed both research and practice. Freeman’s career reflects a blend of leadership in shaping **software engineering education**, rigorous theoretical work on <u>design</u> and <u>reusability</u>, and engagement with broader *science policy* questions affecting computing as a discipline. For more information and a complete list of publications, consult his homepage: http://www.cc.gatech.edu/people/peter-freeman.

2--dZP0AAAAJ.keywords: Equity in CS Education, Coaching inclusive pedagogy, Broadening Participation in Computing, AP CS, AP Computer Science Principles, K-12 Computer Science Education, Instructional coaching, Equity-focused curriculum design, CS education policy, Performance-based assessment in CS
2--dZP0AAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Lien Diaz is the **Director** of the **Constellations Center for Equity in Computing** at **Georgia Tech**, where she leads research and practice at the intersection of computer science education and social justice. Her work centers on *designing and evaluating interventions that expand access to computing* and on coaching educators to implement **inclusive instructional approaches**. Across scholarship and programmatic initiatives she examines <u>equity in CS education</u>, the role of the **Advanced Placement (AP) Computer Science Principles** course in shaping curriculum and policy, and strategies for <u>broadening participation</u> through <u>inclusive pedagogy</u> and targeted *teacher coaching*.

---

## Research Areas
Diaz’s research spans K–12 and transitional pathways into higher education with a sustained focus on structural and pedagogical levers that affect who participates in computing. She has studied the conception, development, and early impacts of the AP Computer Science Principles (CSP) initiative (e.g., work on the development and evolution of the course and its community), investigated whether and how AP CSP can drive equitable curricular and policy change (2024 study), and contrasted AP CSP with AP CS A and first‑year computing concerns (tracking and course alignment). Her scholarship also addresses practical mechanisms for change—teacher *coaching* and professional learning to support equitable CS instruction, leadership models and advisory structures for broadening participation, equity‑focused research methodology for K–12 CS, and critical examinations of allyship and advocacy for women and racially minoritized students in computing. Recent projects extend to K–5 equity frameworks and collaborations between universities and K–12 systems to scale sustainable, inclusive instructional practices.

---

## Notable Works
- "Does the Advanced Placement Computer Science (CS) Principles course drive equitable and inclusive CS pedagogy, curriculum, and policy as a means to broaden participation in …" (2024) — Empirical and policy‑oriented analysis of AP CSP’s capacity to transform classroom practice and expand access for historically underrepresented students.
- "Going beyond the platitudes of equity: Developing a shared vision for equity in computer science education" (2019) — Conceptual and community‑building piece that proposes concrete frameworks and shared language for operationalizing equity across research, teacher preparation, and curriculum.
- "CS principles: development and evolution of a course and a community" (2013) — Foundational account of the AP CSP project’s design choices, community development, and implications for broadening participation and introductory computing pedagogy.

---

## Academic Background
As Director of the Constellations Center for Equity in Computing at Georgia Tech, Diaz combines applied program leadership with a consistent publication record in CS education venues (including SIGCSE and College Board collaborative reports). Her corpus (totaling 62 citations) documents long‑term engagement with the AP CS initiative, K–12/university partnerships, coaching models for teachers, and equity‑driven curricular frameworks. Diaz has contributed to national conversations about CS for All, participated in cross‑sector pilots (e.g., service‑learning and curriculum module partnerships), and authored guidelines for conducting equity‑focused, high‑quality K–12 CS research. Her career reflects sustained collaboration with professional societies and curriculum developers to move research into practice and to institutionalize strategies that broaden participation in computing.

56fOepEAAAAJ.keywords: Data Structures, Algebraic Algorithms, Convex Optimization, Linear Programming, Interior Point Methods, Minimum-Cost Flow, Dynamic Graph Algorithms, Algebraic Data Structures, Spectral Sparsification, Dynamic Linear Algebra
56fOepEAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Jan van den Brand is a faculty member in the College of Computing at **Georgia Tech**, where he leads a research group working at the intersection of **algorithms**, *data structures*, and **optimization** (see his homepage: https://jvdbrand.com/). His work centers on developing fundamentally faster algorithms for core problems in theoretical computer science, with a particular emphasis on <u>algebraic and dynamic data structures</u>, <u>interior‑point and convex optimization methods</u>, and their application to combinatorial problems such as flows and matchings. Much of his research combines rigorous *theory* with careful algorithmic engineering to push classical barriers — for example, by designing solvers and dynamic routines that match or approach the best known bounds from matrix multiplication and spectral methods.

---

## Research Areas
Jan’s research spans several tightly connected areas: the design of near‑linear and almost‑linear time algorithms for optimization problems (notably linear programming and minimum‑cost flow), the development of *dynamic algebraic data structures* for maintaining linear algebraic quantities under updates, and the algorithmic foundations of modern large‑scale learning methods. Representative topics include deterministic and randomized interior‑point methods for linear programs (see "A deterministic linear program solver in current matrix multiplication time" and "Solving tall dense linear programs in nearly linear time"), nearly‑linear algorithms for minimum‑cost flows and related graph optimization problems ("Minimum cost flows, MDPs, and ℓ1‑regression in nearly linear time for dense instances"; "A deterministic almost-linear time algorithm for minimum-cost flow"), fast algorithms for bipartite matching on moderately dense graphs ("Bipartite matching in nearly-linear time on moderately dense graphs"), and a broad program of *dynamic* algorithms — from dynamic matrix inverse routines ("Dynamic matrix inverse: Improved algorithms and matching conditional lower bounds") to fully‑dynamic sparsifiers and distance oracles ("Fully-dynamic graph sparsifiers against an adaptive adversary"; "Sensitive distance and reachability oracles for large batch updates"). He also explores connections to machine learning and systems, including faster training algorithms for overparameterized neural networks ("Training (overparametrized) neural networks in near-linear time") and algorithmic models for attention maintenance in large language models.

## Notable Works
- "A deterministic linear program solver in current matrix multiplication time" — introduced a deterministic interior‑point solver whose runtime matches current matrix multiplication bounds for broad classes of linear programs, reshaping the landscape of high‑precision LP solvers and connecting convex optimization to fast linear algebra.
- "Minimum cost flows, MDPs, and ℓ1‑regression in nearly linear time for dense instances" — developed randomized algorithms that significantly improve runtimes for minimum‑cost flow and related optimization problems on dense graphs, with implications for combinatorial optimization and Markov decision processes.
- "Bipartite matching in nearly-linear time on moderately dense graphs" — gave near‑linear time randomized algorithms for maximum cardinality bipartite matching and several related problems on moderately dense graphs, advancing classical matching algorithms and their practical scalability.

## Academic Background
Jan’s body of work grew out of rigorous doctoral and early‑career research in algorithms, culminating in a 2021 thesis‑length treatment of dynamic matrix algorithms ("Dynamic Matrix Algorithms and Applications in Convex and Combinatorial Optimization") and a steady stream of publications in top venues (STOC/FOCS/SODA and related conferences). He is currently affiliated with **Georgia Tech** and collaborates widely with leading research groups in theory and optimization. His papers have accrued over *1,500 citations* across topics ranging from dynamic graph algorithms to convex optimization, reflecting both depth and broad impact. Jan’s research has been recognized through frequent invitations to premier conferences and workshops and through sustained adoption of his techniques in subsequent work on fast solvers, dynamic data structures, and algorithmic optimization.

6WlU86IAAAAJ.keywords: Research areas not specified, Observational Health Data Sciences and Informatics (OHDSI), OMOP Common Data Model, Electronic Health Record (EHR) data standardization, Synthetic EHR generation (GANs, VAEs), Clinical natural language processing (NLP) and medical coding, Pharmacovigilance and drug–drug interactions, Comparative effectiveness and real-world evidence, Clinical decision support and alert design, Data quality assessment and interoperability (FHIR)
6WlU86IAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Jon Duke is a faculty researcher at **Georgia Tech** who leads a research group working at the intersection of clinical informatics, machine learning, and health-data engineering. His work centers on building scalable, reproducible infrastructure and methods for generating and analyzing real-world clinical data — with particular emphasis on **Observational Health Data Sciences and Informatics (OHDSI)**-based network science, **electronic health records (EHR)** interoperability, and privacy-preserving data generation. He combines *computational methods* (including generative models and explainable machine learning) with pragmatic clinical and public-health applications to improve safety and decision support. Key themes in his program include <u>data standardization using the OMOP common data model</u>, <u>synthetic EHR generation</u>, and <u>explainable clinical prediction and decision-support systems</u>.

---

## Research Areas
Duke’s research spans several tightly connected areas: large-scale observational health research and networked analyses (driven by the OHDSI community), methods and tooling for transforming heterogeneous clinical data into common data models (OMOP CDM) and assessing data quality (e.g., extensions to Achilles), and development of privacy-aware synthetic data generators for EHRs using deep generative models (GANs, conditional VAEs). He also works on explainable natural-language processing for clinical coding and phenotype extraction, clinical decision support and visualization to reduce alert fatigue and improve DDI (drug–drug interaction) contextualization, and comparative-effectiveness / safety studies conducted across multinational observational databases (for example, large-scale antihypertensive and β‑blocker studies). Across these areas he emphasizes open-source, reproducible pipelines and rigorous methodological benchmarking to support trustworthy real-world evidence.

---

## Notable Works
- "Observational Health Data Sciences and Informatics (OHDSI): opportunities for observational researchers" (2015) — influential community and infrastructure paper that helped establish the OHDSI vision and catalyzed multinational, reproducible observational research across standardized EHR/claims networks.  
- "Generating multi-label discrete patient records using generative adversarial networks" (2017) — a high-impact methodological contribution demonstrating how **generative adversarial networks (GANs)** can produce realistic, multi-label synthetic EHR datasets to enable data sharing while mitigating privacy risk.  
- "Explainable prediction of medical codes from clinical text" (2018) — work on interpretable NLP models for mapping clinical notes to diagnostic and procedure codes, advancing practical, explainable ML for clinical coding and downstream decision support.

---

## Academic Background
Jon Duke is based at Georgia Tech and is a prominent contributor to collaborative, multidisciplinary projects linking academic informatics groups, clinical partners, and large observational networks (notably OHDSI and collaborations with institutes such as Regenstrief). His publication record spans methodological machine learning for health, clinical decision support, pharmacovigilance, and large-scale comparative-effectiveness research; his work has accrued over **6,471 citations**, reflecting substantial influence in both informatics methods and applied clinical studies. Duke’s portfolio emphasizes open-source tool development, community standards (OMOP CDM adoption and data-quality tooling), and multinational observational studies; through these activities he has established a leadership role in advancing reproducible real-world evidence and privacy-aware data sharing.

DKiiFh4AAAAJ.keywords: Machine Learning, Multiagent Systems, Computer Vision, Robotics, Collective Animal Behavior, Agent-based Simulation, Multi-target Tracking, Social Structure Inference, Swarm Robotics, Imitation Learning
DKiiFh4AAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Brian Hrolenok is an **Assistant Professor (teaching)** in the Department of **Computer Science** at **George Mason University** (homepage: https://cs.gmu.edu/~hrolenok/). He leads work at the intersection of *machine learning*, *multiagent systems*, and *robotics*, with strong applied ties to **computer vision** and animal behavior modeling. His primary research focus centers on the development of methods for <u>multiagent coordination</u>, <u>automated inference of social and collective behavior from tracking data</u>, and the construction of <u>executable agent-based models</u> from real-world observations that can be used for simulation, analysis, and robotic application.

---

## Research Areas
Hrolenok’s research spans several closely connected areas: principled approaches to multiagent coordination (including sparse environmental signaling mechanisms such as movable beacons as an alternative to pheromone-like communication; see "Collaborative foraging using beacons" (2010)), probabilistic and sampling-based methods for reproducing stochastic collective behaviors ("Sampling beats fixed estimate predictors for cloning stochastic behavior in multiagent systems" (2017)), and the automated learning of executable simulations from live observations ("Learning Executable Models of Multiagent Behavior from Live Animal Observation" (2013); "Constructing and Evaluating Executable Models of Collective Behavior" (2018)). He has applied computer vision and tracking technologies (RFID, continuous 3D tracking, remote video) to infer social structure and dominance in animal groups, particularly rhesus macaques ("Use of position tracking to infer social structure in rhesus macaques" (2018); "Inferring social structure and dominance relationships between rhesus macaques using RFID tracking data" (2014); "USING CONTINUOUS 3D TRACKING AND REMOTE VIDEO TO ENHANCE MANAGEMENT OF SOCIAL GROUPS OF RHESUS MONKEYS" (2022)). His work frequently bridges machine learning, multiagent simulation (notably the MASON simulation ecosystem), and ethology to produce tools that are both analytically rigorous and practically useful for biologists and roboticists.

---

## Notable Works
- "Collaborative foraging using beacons" (2010) — Introduced a sparse, physical-beacon based representation for environmental signaling as a practical substitute for pheromone communication in multiagent coordination, demonstrating robust coordination without direct agent-to-agent messaging.  
- "Learning Executable Models of Multiagent Behavior from Live Animal Observation" (2013) — Developed methods to convert observational data into executable agent-based models, enabling automated construction of simulations that preserve behaviorally relevant dynamics for biological study and hypothesis testing.  
- "Sampling beats fixed estimate predictors for cloning stochastic behavior in multiagent systems" (2017) — Provided a probabilistic framing and empirical demonstration that sampling-based predictors better reproduce stochastic aspects of collective behavior than fixed-estimate approaches, improving fidelity when cloning complex social dynamics.

---

## Academic Background
Brian Hrolenok is a teaching-focused faculty member in George Mason University’s Computer Science department, with a scholarly record that emphasizes interdisciplinary collaboration between computer science, robotics, and biological sciences. His publication history reflects sustained contributions to the MASON multiagent simulation community (including community recommendations in "Future MASON Directions: Community Recommendations" (2013)) and to applied methods for tracking and inferring social structure in animal groups. Over his career his work has accrued a total of approximately **66 citations**, indicating influence across machine learning, multiagent systems, and computational ethology. In his role at George Mason he combines pedagogy with active research and mentorship of undergraduate and graduate students, and he collaborates regularly with domain scientists in biology and robotics to translate sensing and learning advances into usable simulation and analysis tools.

E2Idm8UAAAAJ.keywords: Design, Product, Education, Engineering, Human-Computer Interaction, Assistive Technology, Accessibility, Rehabilitation Technology, Accessible Computing, Peer Review
E2Idm8UAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Mahender Mandala is a researcher based at the **Georgia Institute of Technology** whose work sits at the intersection of **design**, **product development**, *engineering education*, and **assistive technologies**. His scholarship and practice are organized around creating and evaluating inclusive, usable systems for people with disabilities and embedding those design practices into educational contexts. Mahender collaborates with interdisciplinary teams spanning clinical researchers, educators, and technologists, and focuses on <u>accessible design</u>, <u>assistive technology</u>, and <u>human–computer interaction</u> to advance both *practical products* and *pedagogical approaches*.

---

## Research Areas
Mahender’s research portfolio spans several complementary areas: human–computer interaction with an emphasis on disability studies (exemplified by his work on “Articulations toward a crip HCI”), rehabilitation technology and exergames for motor recovery (notably investigations of stroke survivors’ perceptions of technology and VR/AR-based rehabilitation), and engineering/design education practices that scale feedback and learning (including multiple studies on peer review and collaborative feedback in design courses). He also investigates accessibility in computing education for K–12 learners with sensory disabilities (work on accessible computer science for students with hearing impairments and accessible block-based programming for students who are blind/low vision). Additional projects reflect applied engineering and clinical measurement concerns — from mobility and discharge prediction using AM-PAC 6-Clicks in ICU populations to device- and product-level design such as mobile drive-train concepts and dynamic seating systems for pressure-relief. Across these threads, his work emphasizes user-centered evaluation, inclusive pedagogy, and translational collaborations that connect classroom practice, clinical outcomes, and product design.

---

## Notable Works
- “Articulations toward a crip HCI” (2021) — frames HCI through disability-informed critique and practice, arguing for a sustained engagement with disability perspectives to reshape sociotechnical design methods and priorities in the field.
- “Perceptions of stroke survivors regarding factors affecting adoption of technology and exergames for rehabilitation” (2023) — provides empirical insight into user acceptance, barriers, and motivators for adopting exergames and VR/AR in motor rehabilitation, informing design choices for patient-centered rehabilitation technologies.
- “Impact of collaborative team peer review on the quality of feedback in engineering design projects” (2018) — evaluates how collaborative peer-review models affect feedback quality and student learning in large design classes, contributing evidence-based approaches to scalable design education.

---

## Academic Background
Mahender’s publication record (total citations: **142**) indicates long-standing engagement with rehabilitation science, assistive technology, and engineering design education. His work shows sustained collaborations with clinical and rehabilitation research groups (e.g., studies on AM-PAC mobility scores and pressure-relief seating) and with programs that bridge academia and community (service-learning efforts with veterans and teachers, NSF-style RET/REV/T initiatives). These patterns suggest graduate training and professional experience rooted in engineering and rehabilitation design, with a strong emphasis on interdisciplinary, federally funded educational initiatives and professional communities such as RESNA/NCART. At Georgia Tech he continues to contribute to research, teaching, and outreach that integrate product design, inclusive pedagogy, and clinical translational research.
